title: 第 10 節
en: 3. I am the queen, the gatherer of riches, the knowing, first of those who merit worship; the gods have thus established me in many places, staying with many, entering into many.
4. By me it is that he who sees, he who breathes, he who hears what is spoken, eats food; without knowing it, they rest on me. Hear, one and all! I tell thee what I believe. (?)
Brahman without beginning or end, which is the eternal essence of speech, Is changed into the form of things, like the evolution of the world.
Equally strong is the statement of Madhava himself, Sphotflkhyo niravayavo nityaft sabdo "brahmaiveti, 'The eternal word which is called Sphofa, and does not consist of parts, is indeed Brahman.'
5. I, even 1 myself, say this, what is good for gods, and also for men ; whomsoever • I love, him I make formidable, him I make a Brahman, him a Kishi, him a sage.
6. I bend the bow for Rudra (the storm-god) that his arrow may strike the hater of Brahman ; I make war for the people, I have entered both heaven and earth.
7. I bring forth the (my 1) father (Dyaus) on the summit of this world, my origin is in the waters, in the sea ; from thence I spread over all beings, and touch yonder heaven with my height.
8. I indeed spread forth like the wind, to lay hold on all things, beyond the sky, beyond the earth; such have I become through my greatness.'

I ask, is there any trace in these utterances of the thoughts that led in the end to the conception of the Greek Logos'? There is another hymn (5, 71) which is very obscure and has for the first time been rendered more intelligible by Professor Deussen (A. G. P., p. 148), where we meet with* some important remarks showing that language formed an object of thought even at that early time. But here also there is nothing, as yet, approaching to the conception of the Word as a creative power. We meet with such observations as that words were made in the beginning in order to reveal what before had been hidden. This is, no doubt, an important thought, showing that those who uttered it had not yet ceased, like ourselves, to wonder at the existence of such a thing as language. The struggle for life that is going on among words is alluded to by saying that the wise made speech by mind (Manas), sifting as by a sieve the coarsely ground flour. The power of speech is greatly extolled, and eloqu^nce is celebrated as a precious gift. All men shout when the eloquent man appears, ' holding the assembly subdued or spellbound by his words (Sabhasaha), nay he is supposed to remove all sin and to procure sustenance for his friends. The knowledge of all things or, as Deussen says, the knowledge of the origin of things, is taught by the Brahman.

We meet with passages of a very similar character, in MIND AND SPEECH. 67 various parts of the Brahmanas. One of the most startling is found in a verse inserted in the Purusha-hymn, as given in the Taittiriya-ara?iyaka (III, 13, 17), e I know that great sun-coloured Purusha, when on the verge of darkness, he, the wise, rests5> addressing them, after having thought all forms, and having made their names/ Here we have only to translate forms by efity, and names by \6yoi, and we shall not be very far from the world of thought in which Plato and Aristotle 1 moved.

But although we can discover in this hymn an appreciation of the mysterious nature of speech, we look in vain for the clear and definite idea that language and thought are one, which can be so clearly read in the Greek word Logos, both word and thought, nor do we find more than slight anticipations of the Neo-platonist dogma that the creation of the universe was in reality an utterance of the hidden thoughts and words of the Deity.
Blind and Speech.

The following passages will give some idea of what was thought in India about mind and language and their mutual relation. They may be vague and mystical, but they show at all events that a good deal of thought must have been expended by the early thinkers of India on this problem, the nature of speech and the relation between speech and thought.

$atap. Brahmana VI, r, i: 'Prar/ajoati, after having created the Veda (Brahman, neut.), created the waters out of Vai (speech), for Va/c was his. That was created (sent forth). He then entered the waters with Brahman, i. e. the threefold Veda, and there arose from the water an egg which he touched and commanded to multiply. Then from the egg there arose first Brahman, neut., that is, the threefold Veda/ Parc&avimsa Brahmam XX, 14, 2 : ' Pragrapati alone was this, and Vafc was his own, Vafc as the second. He thought, Let me create (send forth) this Vafc, for she will go and become all this/ 1 See.Deussen, I.e., p. 290, F 2 68 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

£atap. Brahm. VII, 5, a, ai : 'The unborn is V&&, and from V&&Visvakarman (the all-maker) begat living beings/ Brih. JLr. Up. I, 5, 3: 'The Atman consists of speech, mind, and breath. There are also the three worlds ; speech is this world, mind the air, breath the sky. The same are the three Vedas, speech the Big-veda, mind the Yac/ur-veda, bueath the Sama-veda. The same are gods, ancestors, and men, speech the gods, mind the ancestors, breath men, &c/ Brih. Ar. Up. I, J , 2,4 : ' He desired, let a second body be born of me, and he (death or hunger) embraced speech with his mind/ A And ibid. I, 4, 17: 'This world in the beginning was Atman*(Self), alone and lonely. He desired, May I have a wife . . . Manas (mind) is the Self, speech the wife, breath the child/ The same or very similar and often contradictory ideas occur in later works also. Thus we read in Manu I, ai : of the Veda, the several names, works, and conditions of all things/ And to quote but one passage from the MahabMrata, $&nti-parva, 8533 : ' In the beginning Vidy& (knowledge, Sophia) without beginning or end, the divine V&& (speech) of the Vedas, was sent forth by Svayambhft, the selfexistent/ Samkara, when treating of Spho£a 1 (word), of which we shall have to treat further on, quotes from the Brih. Ar. Up. I, a, 4 : 'He with his mind united himself with speech/ and he adds an important verse from some Smriti: 'In the beginning divine V&&, Speech, eternal, without beginning or end, consisting of Veda, was uttered by Svayambhft, from which all activities proceeded ' ;

And again: 'In the beginning Mahesvara shaped from the words of the Veda the names and forms of all beings and the procedure of all activities/ The Laws of Manu, or, more correctly, of the Manavas, the clan of Manu, are no doubt ]ater than the but they often contain old thoughts.
1 Ved. Sutras I, 3, 28.

MIND AND SPEECH. 69 These utterances, to which many more might be added, are certainly vague, and chaotic, and often contradictory, because they sprang from different minds without any prearranged system; but they seem to me to show at all events that thought and language must have occupied the philosophers of India far more than they did the philosophers of Greece, and even in later times those of modern Europe. And if some of them assigned the first place to thought and others to speech, this also serves to show that at all events these early guessers did not accept language simply as a matter of course, as most of our modern philosophers are so apt to do, but tried hard to discover whence it came and what was its true relation to thought. Thus we read in the $atap. Br. I, 4, 5, 8 : c A dispute once took place between Mind and Speech as to which was the better of the two. Both said, "I am excellent." Mind said: " Surely I am better than thou, for thou dost not speak anything that is not understood by me, and since thou art only an imitator of what is done by me and a follower in my wake, I am surely better than thou." Speech said: "Surely I am better than thou, for what thou knowest I make known, I communicate."
' They went to appeal to Pra^apati for his decision, and Prac/apati decided in favour of Mind, &c.'

In the Anugita (p. 262) we read on the contrary: e Then the lord of speech was produced, that lord of speech looks up to the mind. First, verily, are words produced, and the mind runs after them/ Some of the Brahmanic thinkers say in so many words that Speech is Brahman ($atap. Br. II, x, 4, jo, Vag vai Brahma), and the co-existence of Brihas-pati and BrahmaTias-pati could hardly have failed to suggest to them the identity of Brahman and Erih in the sense of speech; just as every thoughtful Greek must have known that there was a reason why Logos meant both word and thought. But that ancient chapter of thought which lies beyond the childhood of all philosophy is for ever lost to us and can be reconstructed by conjectures only, which, though they produce conviction in some minds, cannot be expected to produce the same in all.

Taking into account all these scattered indications, I cannot bring myself to accept the evolution of the various meanings of the word Brahman as elaborated by former scholars. I am particularly reluctant to differ on such a point from Professor Deussen. Professor Deussen holds that Brahman had a ritualistic origin (p. 239), and from prayer came to mean he who is prayed to, the Urgrund der Welt He calls it der zum Heiligen, Gottlichen emporstrebende Wille des Menscken, which is much the same idea to which Both and others have given currency, but which certainly requires a fuller justification. Instead of beginning with the specialised meaning of prayer, whether ritualistic or unpremeditated, and then rising to the object of prayer, I prefer to begin with Brahman as a synonym of Brih in B7i/ihaspati, meaning word or speech, and to admit by the side of it another Brahman, meaning that which utters or drives forth (Pra/cyavayati) or manifests or creates, that which is the universal support (Skambha) or force (Daksha), in fact the Brahman, such as we find it afterwards, whether as a neuter, Brahman, or, for more popular purposes, as a masculine, Brahma *. No* doubt in those dark passages through which words passed silently before they emerged into the full light of literature,- we may often fail to discover the right footsteps of their progress, and we must be prepared for differences of opinion. But the really important point is that on which all scholars agree, by assigning to Brahman the final meaning of TO ov, rd OVTCDS ov, TO VP&TQV K.IVOVV, though, even of those terms, as we shall see, not one corresponds fully and exactly to the character of Brahman as developed in the history of the Indian mind.

' The next word we have to examine is Atman. It is next in importance to Brahman only, and the two together may be called the two pillars on which rests nearly the whole of the edifice of Indian philosophy, more particularly of the Vedanta and Samkhya systems.
1 Taitt. Br. II, 7, 17, i.
ATMAN. 7 1 As early as the time of the Ipastamba-Siitras, that is, at the end of the Vedic period, we read, I, 8, 23, i : —

' The BrahmaTi-a who is wise and recognises all things to be in the Atman, who does not become bewildered when pondering (on it), and who recognises the Atman in every (created) thing, he shines indeed in heaven . . .'

And in the same Sutras, I, 8, 23, 2, we find a definition of Brahman, as the cause of the world, which presupposes, as clearly as possible, the prevalence of Vedantic ideas 1 at the time of the author of this Sutra : —

' He who is intelligence itself and subtler than the thread of the lotus-fibre, He who pervades the universe and who, unchangeable and larger than the earth, contains this universe; He who is different from the knowledge of this world which is obtained by the senses and is identicalwith its objects, possesses the highest (form of absolute knowledge). From him who divides himself, spring all (objective) bodies. He is the primary cause, eternal and unchangeable/ The etymology of Atman is again extremely obscure, probably because it belongs to a pre-Sanskritic, though Aryan stratum of Indian speech. However, there can be little doubt that in the Veda Atman, in several places, still means breath, as in Ev. X, 16, 3, suryam AakshuA gaM^atu, va'tam atmS, words addressed A to a dead person, c May the eye go to the sun, the breath (Atrna) to the wind.3 It then came to mean vital breath, life, and, like the spirit or breath, was frequently used in the sense of what we call soul. In some passages it is difficult to say whether we should translate it by life or by spirit. From soul there is but a small step to Self, and that step is often grammatical rather than real. If in the Atharva-veda IX, 5, 30 we read : —

Atmanam pitaram putram paiitram pitamaham, (Jayam #anitrim mat&ram y£ priyas t£n upa hvaye, we have to translate in English, c Myself, father, son, grand- 1 Yoga and Mimamsa also are mentioned by name in the ApastambaSutras, but not yet as de'flnite systems of philosophy. Cf. I, 8, 23, 5 ; II, 4, 8, 13.

son, grandfather, wife, mother, whoever are dear, — I call upon them.' But Self may here be translated by soul or person also, just as we may say, ' My soul doth magnify the Lord/ instead of ' I magnify the Lord/ Again we read, Rv. IX, 113, i, balfim d&dh&n&h atmani, 'putting strength into oneself/ In the end Atman became the regular pronoun self. I need not go through all the evidence which may be seen in any Sanskrit dictionary1, but we have still to see at what stage in its development Atman became the definite name of the soul or Self within. This transition of meaning in Atman offers a curious parallel to that of As, in Asu and Asti, which we examined before.. There are passages such as Rv. I, 164, 4, bhumySA &suh asrik &tm& kva svit, ' Where was the breath, the blood, the spirit of the world?3 Here Atm& may be rendered by spirit or life. But in other passages Atman signifies simply the inmost nature of anything, and more particularly of man, so that in the end it means much the same as what medieval philosophers would have called the quiddity, or Indian philosophers the IdantS, of things. Thus we read at first &tmanam atman& pasya, esee thy Self by thy Self ; ' &tmaiva hy atmanaA s&kshi, { Self is ' the witness of Self/ In this sense Atman is afterwards used as the name of the highest person, the soul of the world (Param&tman), and we read ($atap. Br. XIV, 5, 5, 15): sa va ayam atma sarvesham^ bhutan&m adhipati/z, sarvesh&m bhut&n&m ragra, ' That Atman is the sovereign of all beings, he is the king of all beings/ Fragr&pati, Brahman, Atman.

We have thus seen three words growing up in the hymns and Br&hmanas of the Veda, Prag&pati, Brahman, and Atman, each of which by itself represents in nuce a whole philosophy or a view of the world. In Pra#&pati we have the admission of a personal and supreme being, a god above all gods, a creator and ruler of the world. He created the primeval waters and rose from them as Hira^yagarbha, 1 See Anthropological Eeligion, pp. 200 seq. * Theosophy, pp. 247 seq., or more recently, Deussen's Geschichte der Philosophie, pp. 324 seq.
zh: 3. 我是女王，財富的聚斂者，有智慧者，值得崇拜者之中最先者；諸神於是在眾多之處安置我，停駐於眾多之地，進入於眾多之中。
4. 是我使得看見者看見，呼吸者呼吸，聽聞所言者聽聞，食用食物者食用；他們不知不覺地依靠我而存在。聽著，各位！我告訴你我所相信的。(?)
梵天（Brahman）無始無終，乃言語之永恆本質，化作萬物之形，如世界之演化。
同樣有力的是馬德哈瓦（Madhava）本人的陳述：Sphotflkhyo niravayavo nityaft sabdo "brahmaiveti，「被稱為斯弗塔（Sphofa）的永恆之言，不由部分組成，實乃梵天（Brahman）本身」。
5. 我，甚至我本人，說出這些話，什麼對於諸神是好的，對於人類也是好的；無論誰，我所愛之人，我使他變得可畏，我使他成為婆羅門（Brahman），他成為剎帝利（Kshi），他成為聖賢。
我為魯陀羅（風暴之神）張弓，使其箭矢能擊中梵天的仇敵；我為人民發動戰爭，我已進入天地兩界。
7. 我在此世界的最高峰帶出我的父親（特尤斯），我的起源在水中、在海洋；從那裡我遍佈於所有生靈之上，以我的高度觸及彼方的天空。
8. 我確實如風般散布開來，以掌握萬物，超越天空，超越大地；我藉由我的偉大而成為如此。

我要問，這些話語中是否有任何蛛絲馬跡，顯示最終導向希臘 Logos（理性原則）觀念的思想？還有另一首讚歌（5, 71），其內容相當晦澀，首次由德森教授（A. G. P., p. 148）使其變得較為可理解，我們在其中遇見一些重要的評述，表明語言甚至在那樣早期就曾成為思想的對象。但在這裡，亦如既往，並無任何東西接近於「語言」作為創造性力量的觀念。我們遇見的是這樣的評述：言語乃在太初被製造出來，以揭示先前隱而不顯的事物。這無疑是一個重要的思想，表明那些發出此言的人，如同我們一樣，對於語言這種事物的存在仍未止息其驚異。言語之間進行的生存鬥爭，係藉由以下方式得以提及：智者以心意（Manas）造就語言，如同以篩子篩粗磨的麵粉一般。語言的力量備受推崇，言辭的優美被慶讚為珍貴的禮物。所有人在雄辯者出現時都呼喊讚歎，以其言詞（Sabhasaha）控制或迷住集會。不僅如此，他更被設想為能消除一切罪孽，並為其友人獲得生計。萬物之知識，或如德森所言，事物起源之知識，乃由婆羅門所傳授。

我們在《婆羅門書》（Brahmanas）的各個部分，特別是在《心與言語》67 中發現許多性質相似的段落。其中最令人驚異的一段出現在插入《普魯沙讚歌》中的一首詩句，如《泰帝里耶‧阿蘭耶卡》（Taittiriya-araṇyaka）（III, 13, 17）所載：「我認識那位偉大的金色普魯沙（Purusha），當他在黑暗的邊緣，那位聰慧者休息時，思慮一切形相之後，並為它們賦予名稱。」在這裡，我們只需將「形相」（forms）譯為「實體」（oὐσία），將「名稱」（names）譯為「邏各斯」（λόγοι），我們便不會離柏拉圖與亞里斯多德所活動的思想世界太遠。
但雖然我們可以在這首讚歌中發現對言語之神祕本質的領悟，我們卻徒然尋覓那種明確而確定的觀念，即語言與思想為一體——這種觀念在希臘文詞彙「邏各斯」（Logos）中表現得如此清晰，該詞既表言語亦表思想——同樣地，我們也找不到超越新柏拉圖主義教義之稍許先聲，該教義主張宇宙的創造實際上乃是隱藏於神祇之心念與言語的一種表述。
盲與言語。
下列段落將使我們對印度人關於心靈、語言及其相互關係的觀點有所認識。這些段落也許含糊而神秘，但它們至少說明了印度早期思想家在這一問題上必然投入了相當的思考——即言語的本質，以及言語與思想之間的關係。

$atap. 《梵書》VI, i：「原人神（Prajâpati）創造了吠陀（梵文，中性詞）之後，從言語（Vâc）中創造了水，因為言語是他的。這被創造（發出）了。他隨後以梵文，亦即三重吠陀進入了水中，於是從水中出現了一個卵，他觸碰了它，並命令它增殖。然後從卵中首先出現了梵文（中性詞），即三重吠陀。」《帕切瓦姆薩梵書》（Parc&avimsa Brahmam）XX, 14, 2：「原人神獨自就是這一切，言語是他自己的，言語乃是第二者。他想道：讓我創造（發出）這言語，因為她將前往並成為一切。」 見 Deussen, 同上，第 290 頁註 2 68　印度哲學。

《坦德羅波奧義書》（Taittirîya Brahm. VII, 5, a）云：「未生者為語音，語音生出毘濕伐迦爾曼（Visvakarman，全能造物者），由之而生眾生。」《白馬奧義書》（Brih. Ar. Up. I, 5, 3）云：「梵我（Atman）由語音、心意與呼吸構成。亦有三界；語音即此世界，心意即大氣，呼吸即天空。此亦即三《吠陀》；語音為《梨俱吠陀》，心意為《夜柔吠陀》，呼吸為《沙摩吠陀》。此亦即諸神、祖先與人類，語音為神，心意為祖先，呼吸為人，等等。」《白馬奧義書》（Brih. Ar. Up. I, 1, 2, 4）又云：「彼願曰，願有第二身生於我，遂以心意擁抱語音。」又同書（I, 4, 17）云：「此世界初始即梵我（Self），獨自孤寂。彼願曰，願我有妻……心意（Manas）即自我，語音為妻，呼吸為子。」同樣或極為相似之觀念，且往往互相矛盾者，亦見於後世著作。如《摩奴法典》（Manu I, 21）所載：「吠陀之名、萬物之功用與狀態。」又僅引《摩訶婆羅多》之《沙提篇》（Mahabharata, Çânti-parva, 8533）一段：「初始時，無始無終之知識（Vidyâ，即智慧）、吠陀之神聖語音（Vâk），由自在者（Svayambhû，自存者）而發出。」商迦羅（Samkara）論及斯弗塔（Sphota，字義）時——此事將另有論述——引《白馬奧義書》（Brih. Ar. Up. I, 2, 4）云：「彼以心意與語音相合。」並附錄一部《傳說》（Smriti）中之重要詩句：「初始時，神聖語音（Vâk），言語，永恆無始無終，由吠陀所構成，由自在者（Svayambhû）所發出，一切活動由是而生。」
又言：「太初之時，大自在天（Mahesvara）從吠陀之言詞塑造出一切有情之名與形，以及一切活動之程序。」《摩奴法論》（The Laws of Manu），或更準確地說，《摩奴族法論》（the Manavas），係摩奴之族之法論，雖無疑晚於吠陀，但其中往往蘊含古老之思想。
吠陀經書 I, 3, 28

這些言述，儘管還可以列舉許多其他例子，確實是模糊、混亂且往往自相矛盾的，因為它們來自不同的心靈，並無預先安排的體系；但在我看來，它們至少表明思想與語言必定遠比在希臘哲學家，甚至在近代歐洲哲學家身上，更加佔據了印度哲學家的關注。而且，如果其中某些人把首要地位賦予思想，另有一些人則賦予言語，這也足以說明這些早期的思辨者無論如何並未如我們大多數現代哲學家所慣常做的那樣，把語言視為理所當然之事，而是竭力探究它的來源，以及它與思想的真正關係。因此，我們在《薩塔帕塔梵書》（Śatap. Br.）第一卷第四、五、八章中讀到：曾經心靈與言語之間發生過一場爭論，以確定二者孰優孰劣。兩者都說：「我才是卓越的。」心靈說：「我確實比你優越，因為你所說的任何事物都不超越我的理解，而且由於你不過是我所為之事的模仿者，是我的跟隨者，我確實比你優越。」言語說：「我確實比你優越，因為你所知之事，我將其彰顯出來，我將其傳達出去。」
他們去懇求普拉賈帕蒂（Prajāpati）做出裁決，普拉賈帕蒂裁定心識（Mind）勝訴，等等。

在《阿紐吉塔》(Anugita)（第262頁）中，我們反而讀到：「那時言語之主被創造出來，那言語之主仰望心靈。首先，言語確實被創造出來，而心靈跟隨其後。」有些婆羅門思想家明言言語即梵天（Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa II, x, 4; Vāg vai Brahma），而毗訶斯帕蒂（Brihas-pati）與梵天之主（Brahmaṇas-pati）的並存，幾乎不可能不向他們暗示梵天與言語（Brih）在言語意義上的同一性；就如同每一位深思的希臘人必然知道，*Logos*（邏各斯）既意指言詞又意指思想，必有其原因。然而，那個超越一切哲學童年之外的古老思想篇章已經永遠地喪失於我們，只能由猜想來重建，雖然這些猜想能在某些心靈中引發確信，卻不能期望在所有人心中都產生同樣的效果。

考慮到所有這些零散的跡象，我無法接受前人學者所詳細闡述的「梵」(Brahman)一詞各種意義的演變說。我尤其不願在這樣的問題上與德桑(Deussen)教授相左。德桑教授主張梵具有儀式的起源(p. 239)，並認為從祈禱發展而來，得以指稱被祈禱者——世界的根本(Urgrund der Welt)。他稱之為「人對聖聖與神聖所發之至高意志」(der zum Heiligen, Gottlichen emporstrebende Wille des Menscken)，這與波特(Both)及其他人所推行的思想大致相同，但無疑需要更充分的論證。與其從祈禱的專門化意義出發——無論是儀式性的或未經預謀的——而後升進到祈禱的對象，我寧願從梵作為「言」(Brih)的同義詞開始，如在《婆羅賀斯帕蒂》(B7i/ihaspati)中所見，意為言語或語言，並同時承認另一個梵的含義，即發言或驅動者(Pra/cyavayati)，或顯現或創造者，乃至普遍的支柱(Skambha)或力(Daksha)——實際上就是我們後來所發現的梵，無論作為中性的梵(Brahman)，或為更通俗之目的而用的陽性形式梵天(Brahma)*。誠然，在那些言語曾無聲地通過的幽暗通道中，在它們從文獻的全面光照下現身之前，我們往往可能無法發現其進展的確切足跡，我們必須做好見解不同的準備。但真正重要的是所有學者都贊同的一點，即給予梵以最終意義為「存在者」(TO ov, rd OVTCDS ov, TO VP&TQV K.IVOVV)，儘管即使這些術語，如我們將會看到的，其中沒有一個能完全且確切地對應於梵在印度思想史發展中所展現的特性。
我們接下來要檢視的詞彙是「阿特曼」（Atman）。在重要性上，它僅次於「梵」（Brahman），這兩個概念可謂是支撐印度哲學——尤其是吠檀多學派（Vedanta）與數論學派（Samkhya）——幾乎整座思想宏殿的兩根支柱。
泰迪利亞梵書（Taittirīya Brāhmaṇa）II, 7, 17, i。
如同在伊帕斯坦巴經（Îpastamba-Sûtras）時代早期—即吠陀時期之末—我們在第一卷第八章第二十三節讀到的記載：
「聰慧的婆羅門，認識一切事物皆在梵我（Atman）之中，在思量（此事）時不陷於迷茫，且在一切（受造）之物中識得梵我者，彼確實在天界中閃耀...」
在同一部經論第一卷第八篇第二十三段第二部分，我們發現了對梵天（Brahman）的一個定義——將其視為世界之因，這個定義極其明確地顯示出該經論作者時代已經盛行吠檀多（Vedantic）思想：

他即是智慧本身，比蓮纖細線更為幽微者，他遍佈宇宙，不可改變，且大於大地，容納此宇宙；他異於由感官所獲得之此世界知識，且與其客體相同一，具有最高（絕對知識之）形態。自他分裂自身處，生起所有（客觀之）身體。他是原因，永恆且不可改變。阿特曼（Atman）之語源學又甚為晦澀，其原因可能在於它屬於前梵文時期，雖為雅利安的印度語言層。然而，毫無疑問，在吠陀中，阿特曼在若干處仍然意為呼吸，如《梨俱吠陀》X, 16, 3，「suryam akshum gamatu, vayum atma」，此乃對死者所言之詞，*願眼睛往向太陽，呼吸（Atma）往向風。*其後演變為意指生命呼吸，生命，且如靈或呼吸，常被用以表示我們所謂之靈魂。在某些段落中，難以判定我們應將其譯為生命或靈。自靈魂至自我，只有一小步之遠，而那一步往往較多為語法性而非實質性。若在《雅茹瓦吠陀》（Atharva-veda）IX, 5, 30中我們讀到：

我注意到這段文本存在以下問題： 1. **梵文部分殘缺且排版混亂**（"Atmanam pitaram putram paiitram pitamaham, (Jayam #anitrim mat&ram y£ priyas t£n upa hvaye"）——包含明顯的印刷錯誤和不完整的轉錄 2. **英文翻譯部分未完成**（"we have to translate in English, c Myself, father, son, grand- 1"）——句子被中斷 3. **缺乏完整的上下文**——無法判斷這段文字的確切出處和含義 基於現有的可讀部分，我的翻譯如下： 瑜伽（Yoga）和彌曼差（Mimamsa）在《阿帕斯坦巴經書》（Apaśtamba-Sūtras）中也被提及名字，但尚未作為明確的哲學體系出現。參見 I, 8, 23, 5；II, 4, 8, 13。 **建議**：請提供清晰的原文掃描件或完整的句子，以便進行準確翻譯。

兒子、祖父、妻子、母親，凡是親愛的人——我呼喚他們。」但在此處，自我（Self）也可譯為靈魂或人格，正如我們可說「我的靈魂頌讚主」而非「我頌讚主」。再者，我們讀到《梨俱吠陀》第九卷第113頌第1節，*balfim dādhānāh atmani*，「將力量注入自我」。最終，阿特曼（Atman）遂成為規律的自我代詞。毋庸逐一論述梵文詞典中可見的所有證據，然而我們仍需了解在其發展的何種階段，阿特曼才成為靈魂或自我的確切名稱。阿特曼在意義上的此種轉變，與我們先前檢視的阿斯（As）、以及在阿蘇（Asu）和阿斯蒂（Asti）中的轉變，呈現奇異的平行現象。有些段落如《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第164頌第4節，*bhumy&A asuh asrik &tm& kva svit*，「世界的呼吸、血液、精神在何處？」此處阿特摩（Atm&）可譯為精神或生命。然而在其他段落中，阿特曼單純表示任何事物的最內在本質，特別是人的本質，因此最終其意義大致相當於中世紀哲學家所稱的「本質性」（quiddity），或印度哲學家所稱的「此性」（idantā）。因此我們最初讀到*ātmānam ātman& paśya*，「以自我觀自我」；*ātmaiva hy ātmanaA sākshi*，「自我乃自我之見證人」。按此義，阿特曼嗣後被用作最高人格、世界靈魂（帕拉瑪特曼）的名稱，我們在《沙達帕塔梵書》第十四卷第五章第5、15節讀到：*sa va ayam ātma sarvesham^ bhūtānām adhipati*，*sarvesh&m bhūtānām rāgra*，「此阿特曼乃一切眾生之主權者，他乃一切眾生之王」——帕尼帕提（Pragr&pati）、梵（Brahman）、阿特曼。

我們因此看到了三個詞在吠陀的讚歌和梵書中逐漸發展成形——普拉賈帕蒂（Pragāpati）、梵（Brahman）和阿特曼（Atman）——每一個都本身代表了一整套哲學或一種世界觀。在普拉賈帕蒂中，我們看到了對一個人格的、至高的存在者的承認，一位超越所有諸神的神，一位世界的創造者與統治者。他創造了原始的水體，並以金胎（Hiraṇyagarbha）的名義從中升起。¹ ¹ 見《人類學宗教》，頁200以下。＊《神智學》，頁247以下，或近作德伊森《哲學史》，頁324以下。
